beats.n_arrival: {HERO} cuts the padlock on {ENTRANCE} of the {THRESHOLD} the night before the wrecking {DEADLINE_SIGN}s arrive; her late aunt {KIN} died a patient here and {GOAL} are somewhere inside; a demolition {WATCHER} does a round every hour; {HERO} has a headlamp, a folding knife, and until {DEADLINE}; the rain is coming and the great house waits, dark and breathing, its central {HUB_NAME} the only room she remembers from the day they took {KIN} away
beats.n_hub: the central {HUB_NAME} of {THRESHOLD}, a domed hall where every wing corridor opens off a ring of dead doorways; from here {HERO} can reach the flooded west stairwell, the hydrotherapy hall, the cold rooms, the chapel crypt, the boiler house, the superintendent's wing, the locked dormitories, the dispensary, and the attic wards, or turn back for {ENTRANCE}; write this so it reads on a first arrival or on any return between corners, the {WATCHER}'s hourly torch a distant sweep across the far windows
beats.e_abandon: {HERO} stands in the {HUB_NAME} listening to the rain find its way through the dome and decides the dead cannot be helped by the dead; she walks out through {ENTRANCE} empty-handed as the first {DEADLINE_SIGN} trucks turn in, and reads about the demolition in the paper a week later, {KIN}'s name uncleared and the question closed with the building
beats.n_stair_r1: {HERO} enters {STAIR_NAME}: the west stairwell drops into standing black water that laps the seventh step; a rope of knotted sheets hangs from the dark above, and the ledger room {HERO} wants is rumoured to be at the very top of {THRESHOLD}; then {HERO} wades the flooded landing, cold to the knee, feeling for the submerged rail with the toe of her boot
beats.n_stair_r2: a drowned nurses' station, its pigeonholes furred with silt, a brass call-bell board still wired to every ward
beats.n_stair_r3: the half-collapsed second landing where the banister has torn free and leans out over the stairwell void
beats.n_stair_r4: a linen chute {HERO} could climb if she trusts the rusted brackets to hold her weight
beats.n_stair_r5: the third-floor corridor, dry at last, wallpaper hanging in long wet tongues from the ceiling
beats.n_stair_r6: a bricked-up doorway with fresh mortar; someone sealed this recently and {HERO} wonders what is behind it
beats.n_stair_r7: the seized fire door that must be shouldered open a stubborn inch at a time
beats.n_stair_r8: a gallery of framed benefactors, every face scratched out with the same patient knife
beats.n_stair_r9: the fourth landing where the stairs simply end in open air, the flight above long since burned away
beats.n_stair_r10: a jury-rigged ladder of shelving lashed together against the shaft wall, climbing toward the fifth floor
beats.n_stair_r11: the fifth-floor archive annex, its shelves swept bare but for a single index card box
beats.n_stair_r12: a narrow maintenance passage between the walls, hot and close and loud with roosting pigeons
beats.n_stair_r13: the sixth landing, where a wheelchair sits facing the wall as though its occupant just stood and left
beats.n_stair_r14: the last true staircase, marble worn to a shine by decades of feet, spiralling up to the tower
beats.n_stair_r15: the tower antechamber, a ring of locked patient cells and one door standing open on darkness
beats.n_stair_r16: the threshold of the records tower, and behind its warped door the smell of old paper and something turned
beats.n_stair_term: the sealed records tower at the top of {THRESHOLD}; here the patient ledgers are shelved by year, and {KIN}'s volume waits with the truth {HERO} came for, if she can carry it back down before the {WATCHER} or the {DEADLINE} or the failing floor ends her night
beats.e_stair_1: {HERO} finds {KIN}'s volume, tucks it under her coat, and makes the long descent back through the flooded stair to {ENTRANCE} as the first {DEADLINE_SIGN} truck grinds up the drive; the record is out, the name will be cleared, and {THRESHOLD} can fall now that its worst secret is on its way to daylight
beats.e_stair_2: the ledger for {KIN}'s year is here but her pages have been razored out clean; {HERO} photographs the stubs and the razor marks and leaves knowing exactly who to accuse, even without the words, one certainty carried down in place of the proof
beats.n_hydro_r1: {HERO} enters {HYDRO_NAME}: the hydrotherapy hall, a long vault of cracked green tile where iron tubs sit in rows under seized brass taps and canvas restraint straps rotted to threads; then {HERO} walks the row of tubs, each still ringed with a tide-line of mineral scale at a different, deliberate height
beats.n_hydro_r2: the plunge pool, drained but for black rainwater, a swimmer's ladder descending into it and no bottom visible
beats.n_hydro_r3: a wall of pressure gauges frozen at red, and the great intake pipe that once fed scalding and freezing water on command
beats.n_hydro_r4: the attendants' alcove, a logbook open on the desk, the last entry a single word underlined three times
beats.n_hydro_r5: a store of numbered canvas wraps folded with terrible neatness on floor-to-ceiling shelves
beats.n_hydro_r6: the pump gallery, its floor a lattice of open drainage grates over a sound of running water far below
beats.n_hydro_r7: a side room of hip-baths where the taps drip in an almost patient rhythm despite the mains being long cut
beats.n_hydro_r8: the treatment ledger cabinet, its drawers labelled by symptom, most hanging open and rifled already
beats.n_hydro_r9: a jammed grate where a length of pale cloth has snagged and streams in the unseen current below
beats.n_hydro_r10: the far end of the hall where the tiles give way to bare, sweating brick and the temperature drops hard
beats.n_hydro_r11: a maintenance crawlway under the tubs, {HERO}'s headlamp catching the glint of dropped instruments in the muck
beats.n_hydro_r12: the electrotherapy annex, a rubber mat, a dial, and two frayed leads coiled on a hook by the tubs
beats.n_hydro_r13: the sluice room where a single valve wheel stands proud, freshly greased in a building where nothing else has been touched
beats.n_hydro_term: the sluice control room above the drowned sub-basement; the greased valve here empties or floods the whole lower hall, and a demolition charge sits taped to the intake pipe with a {DEADLINE_SIGN} tag {HERO} was not meant to see
beats.e_hydro_1: the tag proves the {DEADLINE_SIGN} pre-set charges days early, before the required survey; {HERO} pockets the tag as evidence of corner-cutting and slips out, the ledgers still unfound but a different scandal now in her hand
beats.e_hydro_2: the greased valve was a trap; the door drops behind {HERO} on a counterweight and the room begins to fill from the intake she opened, water at her ankles, then her knees, and no wheel on this side to turn it back before the {DEADLINE_SIGN} find her at {DEADLINE}
beats.e_hydro_3: {HERO} tugs the taped charge to read the {DEADLINE_SIGN} tag and the corroded trembler switch does the rest; the intake pipe lets go in a white hammer of pressure and the hydrotherapy hall becomes her grave hours before the {DEADLINE_SIGN}s would have brought it down themselves
beats.n_morgue_r1: {HERO} enters {MORGUE_NAME}: the mortuary corridor, its double doors swinging on a draught that should not exist, the air ten degrees colder than the rest of {THRESHOLD} and smelling of old formalin; then {HERO} passes the drawer wall, thirty steel doors, one of them ajar and the runner drawn out a hand's width into the dark
beats.n_morgue_r2: the preparation room where an enamel table tilts to a central drain and the overhead lamp sways though the air is still
beats.n_morgue_r3: a shelf of specimen jars gone amber and opaque, their handwritten labels curling off in the cold
beats.n_morgue_r4: the register desk with its book of the dead, columns of names in one clerk's cramped hand across forty years
beats.n_morgue_r5: a walk-in cold store, door propped by a brick, its shelves stripped but for a stack of numbered toe tags
beats.n_morgue_r6: the pathologist's office, a coat still on the hook and a wristwatch on the blotter stopped at a quarter to three
beats.n_morgue_r7: a passage of glazed brick where {HERO}'s own breath smokes and the headlamp beam seems to reach less far than it should
beats.n_morgue_r8: the incinerator antechamber, its firebox door standing open on a bed of grey ash and small unburned things
beats.n_morgue_r9: a records niche where mortuary ledgers are bricked in behind a grille, reachable only through the bent lower corner
beats.n_morgue_r10: the far cold room where the temperature bottoms out and {HERO}'s fingers start to stop obeying her
beats.n_morgue_r11: a service lift shaft, its car stalled between floors, the only way up a maintenance ladder in the freezing dark
beats.n_morgue_r12: the threshold of the death register vault, where the tally of who {THRESHOLD} killed is kept in the coldest room of all
beats.n_morgue_r13: a final antechamber lined with drawer-cards, each a life reduced to a date, a diagnosis, and a discharge that never came
beats.n_morgue_term: the death register vault, kept cold to preserve the paper; here the true cause-of-death book contradicts every polished official record, and {KIN}'s entry is somewhere in these frozen stacks if {HERO} can read it before the cold reads her
beats.e_morgue_1: {HERO} finds the true entry: {KIN} did not die of the fever {THRESHOLD} claimed; she copies the page by headlamp, hands shaking with cold and rage, and carries one damning photograph out into the rain, enough to reopen everything
beats.e_morgue_2: absorbed in the register, {HERO} does not hear the {WATCHER} until his torch pins her against the drawer wall; he is calling it in already, and there is no back way out of a vault with one iron door and her name now on a police report
beats.e_morgue_3: the cold store door swings shut on its brick and the latch takes; {HERO} works at the hinge until her fingers will not close, then sits, then does not get up, found in the spring thaw by the salvage men clearing the last of the steel
beats.n_chapel_r1: {HERO} enters {CHAPEL_NAME}: the deconsecrated chapel, its roof half open to the coming storm, pews shoved to the walls and the altar stone dragged aside over a black rectangle of stair; then {HERO} crosses the nave under a rose window with every pane knocked out, rain beginning to spit through onto the flags
beats.n_chapel_r2: the vestry, cassocks rotted on their pegs, a strongbox forced open long ago and empty but for a brass key
beats.n_chapel_r3: the choir loft reached by a ladder of missing rungs, its organ pipes torn out for the metal
beats.n_chapel_r4: a memorial wall of small brass plaques, patient numbers only, no names, hundreds of them green with age
beats.n_chapel_r5: the crypt stair beneath the altar, descending into an under-church smell of wet stone and lime
beats.n_chapel_r6: the burial register lectern, its book chained to the stand, open to a page of pauper interments in numbered plots
beats.n_chapel_r7: the pauper vault, brick loculi stacked to the ceiling, most sealed, a few gaping and swept clean
beats.n_chapel_r8: a lime store where the sacks have burst and drifted white across the floor like a dry, caustic snow
beats.n_chapel_r9: the sexton's nook, a spade and a plot map and a tin of the numbered brass tags that match the plaques above
beats.n_chapel_r10: the far crypt where the floor has subsided and a run of graves stands half unearthed by the settling ground
beats.n_chapel_r11: the bell-rope closet, its rope cut short at head height, the bell above silenced with a knotted rag
beats.n_chapel_r12: a bricked recess with newer mortar, and behind it, when {HERO} knocks, the flat sound of a filled cavity
beats.n_chapel_term: the crypt's plot ledger and the sexton's map together, which match {THRESHOLD}'s nameless numbered graves to the patients who were never reported dead; {KIN}'s number is on the map, and the freshly bricked recess is exactly where it points
beats.e_chapel_1: the map ties {KIN}'s patient number to an unrecorded grave; {HERO} photographs map, ledger, and plaque together, the chain of proof complete, and climbs back into the storm with a truth the wrecking ball cannot bury now
beats.e_chapel_2: the storm comes through the open roof in a sheet and the crypt stair turns to a running gutter; {HERO} is forced up and out with her photographs half taken, the graves' secret intact for one more night that she may not get again
beats.e_chapel_3: {HERO} pulls the newer bricks to see the cavity and the old mortar above lets go, the recess collapsing to pen her in a space too small to turn, calling into a chapel where only the {WATCHER} will ever hear
beats.n_boiler_r1: {HERO} enters {BOILER_NAME}: the boiler house, a cathedral of cold iron where three great furnaces stand dead and a maze of lagged pipes climbs into the roof dark, asbestos hanging in grey rags; then {HERO} threads the pipe maze, ducking runs of lagging that shed grey dust into her headlamp beam with every brush
beats.n_boiler_r2: the coal cellar, its bunker half full still, a slide of black rising to a chute that goes up toward the yard
beats.n_boiler_r3: the furnace floor, three firebox doors, one warm to the touch though nothing here has burned in twenty years
beats.n_boiler_r4: the pressure hall of dead gauges and a wheel of stopcocks, one of them turned against all the rest
beats.n_boiler_r5: a fitter's workshop, tools rust-welded to their board, a bench vice gripping a length of bent iron bar
beats.n_boiler_r6: the ash pits, deep and dry, spanned by a single plank a demolition man laid and did not take back
beats.n_boiler_r7: a service tunnel branching toward every wing, the arteries of {THRESHOLD}, warm and lightless and long
beats.n_boiler_r8: the fuel ledger office where deliveries and, oddly, disposals were logged in the same careful column
beats.n_boiler_r9: a wall of valve tags, and among them a fresh {DEADLINE_SIGN} tag reading do not charge until survey in a hand already ignored
beats.n_boiler_r10: the catwalk over the boilers, its grating soft with rust, swaying underfoot above a drop into the ash
beats.n_boiler_r11: the plant control room, glass gone, its master board a diagram of the whole sanatorium's guts
beats.n_boiler_r12: the threshold of the pressure vault where the plant records and the demolition plan are kept in the same steel cabinet
beats.n_boiler_term: the boiler-house control room, whose master board maps every tunnel in {THRESHOLD} and whose steel cabinet holds both the disposal ledger and the {DEADLINE_SIGN}'s early charge plan; from here {HERO} can reach any wing, or bring the whole building down early by accident
beats.e_boiler_1: the catwalk grating gives under {HERO}'s boot mid-span and folds her through into the ash pit below; the fall breaks what needs to break, and {THRESHOLD} keeps her the way it kept the numbered graves, quietly and without a record
beats.e_boiler_2: the fuel ledger's disposal column was never about coal; {HERO} reads what was fed to the furnaces and by whom, photographs the page, and carries out of the boiler house a proof heavier than any single name
beats.e_boiler_3: {HERO} ducks into the service tunnel to cross to another wing and a fire shutter drops behind her on a corroded fusible link; the far end is bricked and the near end shut, and she is sealed in the warm dark of {THRESHOLD}'s arteries with the trucks not due for hours and no one who knows she is here
beats.n_office_r1: {HERO} enters {OFFICE_NAME}: the administration wing, panelled and grand where the wards were bare, a run of offices climbing to the superintendent's rooms behind a door still bearing his brass nameplate; then {HERO} moves down the clerks' hall, typewriters shrouded in dust sheets like a row of small square ghosts
beats.n_office_r2: the filing registry, its cabinets a canyon of drawers, most emptied in a hurry that left paper on the floor
beats.n_office_r3: the committee room, a long table set as if for a meeting, one chair pulled out and turned to face the door
beats.n_office_r4: the correspondence office, a franking machine and a wire basket of letters never sent, all to the same ministry
beats.n_office_r5: a strongroom with its door ajar, shelves of cash boxes forced and a single locked deed box left behind
beats.n_office_r6: the matron's office, a duty roster on the wall with names inked out one by one down the years
beats.n_office_r7: a private stair to the upper floor, carpeted once, the runner now a black stripe of mould climbing the treads
beats.n_office_r8: the personnel office, staff files fanned across the desk, several with their photographs deliberately removed
beats.n_office_r9: an anteroom of locked glass cases, certificates and commendations, and one empty hook where a portrait hung
beats.n_office_r10: the ledger room where bound minute-books line the walls, a decade missing from the middle of the run
beats.n_office_r11: the archive lift, a hand-cranked dumbwaiter for files, its car jammed with a single grey folder just out of reach
beats.n_office_r12: the typing pool, a dozen desks, one carbon still threaded in a platen with half a name typed on it
beats.n_office_r13: the deeds and estates office, plans of {THRESHOLD}'s grounds pinned up, one plot inked over in red
beats.n_office_r14: the wages office, a run of pay ledgers and, tucked behind them, a private cash book in a different hand
beats.n_office_r15: the superintendent's outer office, a secretary's desk, an appointment book open to the week {KIN} died
beats.n_office_r16: the threshold of the superintendent's study, its door unlocked, a reading lamp on the desk still faintly warm
beats.n_office_term: the superintendent's study, where the private correspondence file explains in his own signed hand what {THRESHOLD} did and hid; the lamp is warm, which means someone was here tonight, and the file {HERO} needs is the one already half in the fire grate
beats.e_office_1: {HERO} pulls the file from the grate before it catches, the superintendent's own signature on every damning line, and is gone through the study window and down the fire escape into the storm with the confession dry against her chest
beats.e_office_2: she is a minute too late; the file is a curl of black flakes and the reading lamp still warm from whoever burned it; {HERO} leaves with nothing but the certainty that someone else is inside {THRESHOLD} tonight and knew exactly what to destroy
beats.n_ward_r1: {HERO} enters {WARD_NAME}: the long dormitory ward, thirty iron beds in two rows under a vaulted ceiling, restraint rings still bolted to the frames and the wind moving the curtains that divide nothing from nothing; then {HERO} walks the aisle of beds, each with a slate at its foot still chalked with a number and a treatment she does not want to read
beats.n_ward_r2: the seclusion rooms, doors of studded iron with a spy-hatch each, one hatch open on a padded dark
beats.n_ward_r3: a day-room where chairs face a wall as if watching a window that was bricked over from outside
beats.n_ward_r4: the patients' effects store, wire baskets of watches, rings, and letters tagged by number and never returned
beats.n_ward_r5: a nurses' corridor with a medicine trolley abandoned mid-round, labelled bottles rolled into the corners
beats.n_ward_r6: the observation gallery above the ward, a glassed box from which one attendant could watch every bed at once
beats.n_ward_r7: a locked side ward, its door forced by someone before {HERO}, the wood splintered outward as if against a push from within
beats.n_ward_r8: the airing court door, chained, beyond it a walled yard of dead grass and a single bench facing the wall
beats.n_ward_r9: a records alcove where ward diaries are stacked, the nightly notes of who cried out and who went quiet
beats.n_ward_r10: the isolation annex, colder and stiller, its four cells numbered in a sequence that skips {KIN}'s
beats.n_ward_r11: the threshold of the ward matron's cabinet, where the treatment authorisations are locked, the paper trail of every night here
beats.n_ward_term: the ward matron's cabinet, holding the signed authorisations for what was done in these beds; {KIN}'s slate and her authorisation are filed together here, and reading them tells {HERO} everything and takes the time she does not have
beats.e_ward_1: there is a lifetime of authorisations here and {HERO} tries to take all of it, and the hour turns, and the {DEADLINE_SIGN} trucks are in the drive before she has chosen; she flees with an armful of the wrong papers and none of the right
beats.e_ward_2: {KIN}'s authorisation carries a signature that should never have been near a patient; {HERO} photographs slate and paper together, one clean link in the chain, and is back to the {HUB_NAME} before her nerve gives out
beats.e_ward_3: the forced side-ward door swings to behind {HERO} on a wind she did not feel and the sprung lock takes; she is inside a room built to hold people who fought harder than she can, and the {WATCHER}'s torch is already sweeping the aisle
beats.n_dispensary_r1: {HERO} enters {DISPENSARY_NAME}: the dispensary, a warren of shelving behind a marble counter, amber bottles racked floor to ceiling and a poison cupboard with its double lock hanging open; then {HERO} edges between shelves of stoppered bottles, their labels a pharmacopoeia of things no one prescribes any more
beats.n_dispensary_r2: the compounding bench, mortar and pestle set out, a residue in the bowl still faintly, wrongly sweet
beats.n_dispensary_r3: the poison register, chained to the counter, its withdrawals logged in grams against ward numbers and dates
beats.n_dispensary_r4: a cold cabinet, its ammonia line long dead, shelves of ampoules clouded and cracked in their trays
beats.n_dispensary_r5: the returns basket where unused doses came back, or should have, the tally never matching the register
beats.n_dispensary_r6: a store of green-glass demijohns too heavy to have been carried out, their contents settled to sludge
beats.n_dispensary_r7: the pharmacist's cubby, a stool, a ledger, and a locked drawer that rattles with something small and loose
beats.n_dispensary_r8: a back passage to the wards, a dumbwaiter still rigged, its rope taut as though a load hangs somewhere in the shaft
beats.n_dispensary_r9: the fume room, its extraction hood dead, a chemical burn ghosting the steel where something boiled dry
beats.n_dispensary_r10: the label press, a drawer of blank apothecary labels and a rubber stamp reading for ward use only
beats.n_dispensary_r11: a narrow bond store, its iron gate ajar, narcotics ledgers stacked and one clean gap on the shelf
beats.n_dispensary_r12: the threshold of the controlled store, twice locked and once forced, where the real withdrawal book is kept apart from the show ledger
beats.n_dispensary_term: the controlled-drugs store behind the forced double lock, where the true withdrawal book records doses the official register never shows; the figures against {KIN}'s ward on the night she died are the proof, if the fumes and the failing air let {HERO} stay to read
beats.e_dispensary_1: the real book shows a dose against {KIN}'s ward that no illness could justify; {HERO} photographs the double columns side by side, the lie and the truth in one frame, and gets out before the fume room's air turns her head for good
beats.e_dispensary_2: {HERO} tips a demijohn to read its faded label and the disturbed sludge gives up a breath of gas that has waited twenty years in the dark; she is on the floor between the shelves before she understands, and the dispensary keeps its register private
beats.n_attic_r1: {HERO} enters {ATTIC_NAME}: the attic stair to the roof-space wards under the great slate gables, where the cheapest patients were kept hottest in summer and coldest in winter, the ceiling following the pitch of the roof; then {HERO} climbs into the roof-space, headlamp finding a low tunnel of iron cots between the rafters and the sloping slates
beats.n_attic_r2: a run of dormer cells, each a wedge of room with a single barred skylight streaked grey with pigeon lime
beats.n_attic_r3: the water-tank loft, a great riveted cistern sweating in the dark, its overflow pipe dripping onto rotten boards
beats.n_attic_r4: a storage bay of tea-chests, patient effects marked for a return address that was always the same graveyard
beats.n_attic_r5: the rafters walkway, a single line of boards laid over lath and plaster, a wrong step waiting to punch through to the ward below
beats.n_attic_r6: an attendants' bothy, a paraffin stove and a cot, a private den for someone who worked the wards no one checked
beats.n_attic_r7: a bay where the roof has failed and the storm is already inside, rain sheeting down a slope of broken slate
beats.n_attic_r8: the bell-turret access, a vertical iron ladder into the little tower that once called the wards to order
beats.n_attic_r9: a hidden room behind a false gable wall, missed by every inventory, its door a slab of plaster on hinges
beats.n_attic_r10: a lumber bay of stacked bed-frames and rolled mattresses, a path worn through them by recent feet
beats.n_attic_r11: the threshold of the attic strongbox, wedged under the ridge beam, where the last superintendent hid what he would not burn
beats.n_attic_term: the hidden attic strongbox under the ridge beam, the one cache the fire and the shredders never found; inside are the photographs and the unedited casebooks that make every other proof unnecessary, and the storm-broken roof is the only way the wind and the {WATCHER} both got in
beats.e_attic_1: {HERO} forces the strongbox and finds the casebooks whole, but the roof is open and the rain is already on them; she wraps what she can in oilcloth and goes down and out with a soaked, half-legible cache, proof and ruin bound in the same bundle, enough to start a reckoning and not enough to be sure of finishing it
beats.e_attic_2: the walkway board gives without warning and {HERO} goes through the ceiling into the ward below in a fall of lath and plaster; the strongbox stays hidden under its beam, and the wrecking {DEADLINE_SIGN}s find her in the morning under a snow of white dust

n_arrival.c_arrival_hub: Cross the overgrown court and step into the {HUB_NAME}.
